metadata.title: Negro Leagues Documented Salary Data, 1920-1948
metadata.methodology_note: Every figure traces to a cited source. Confidence levels: Documented (primary source, e.g. ledger), Verified (multiple independent secondary sources agree), Reported (single secondary source or oral history), Estimated (inferred from context with stated assumptions).

sources.citation: Birmingham Black Barons Account Book, 1926-1930. Joyce Sports Research Collection, Hesburgh Libraries, University of Notre Dame. Digitized via Marble.
Britannica, 'Negro league: The Negro leagues gain prominence.'
Philadelphia Phillies Urban Youth Academy, 'Economics' (Negro Leagues educational program).
Negro Leagues Baseball eMuseum, 'Life As a Black Baseball Player: Playing Conditions.'
Gardner, Robert and Dennis Shortelle. 1993. Cited in NLBM eMuseum player conditions page.
Holway, John B. 1988. Cited in NLBM eMuseum player conditions page.
Wikipedia, 'Satchel Paige.' Cross-referenced against multiple sources.
The Pendergast Years, 'Satchel Paige.' Kansas City historical project.
Larry Lester, SABR Negro Leagues Research Committee. Salary research cited across multiple secondary sources including Phillies UYA and NLBM.
Peterson, Robert. Only the Ball Was White. 1970. Foundational secondary source.
Hayes, James M. 'The Rightness of Negro League Player Reparations.' Institute of the Black World 21st Century, June 2020.

documented_salaries.notes: Ledger also shows debits: $7.50 for shoes (Gray's Sporting Goods), $10.00 and $15.00 to 'Dr. Bradford', $5.00 fine for 'Not appearing in uniform in St. Louis', $10.00 fine for 'Staying out all night at Chicago'.
With bonuses. First full professional contract. Up from $50/month in his first year with Chattanooga.
First year with Birmingham. Some sources report this as his monthly rate.
By far the highest salary in the Negro Leagues at the time. Greenlee acquiesced to Paige's demands.
Played in Dominican Republic for $30,000, on par with the best white major leaguers.

Earned $30,000 to $40,000 per year through special deals, pitching 1-3 innings for independent teams. Four times the pay of the average New York Yankee. Some sources report $500 per game pitched. Reported to be earning approximately $500 per game at the beginning of the 1940s.
Had 55 home runs and 239 RBI and earned $400 for the season. One of the few specific documented wage figures.
Salary in Mexico. Homestead owner Cum Posey filed a $10,000 suit against him. Second-highest paid player in Black baseball behind Paige.

salary_ranges.notes: Monthly average of $230 per Gardner and Shortelle (1993). Journeyman monthly of $150 per Britannica. Range of $100-$400 per Phillies UYA. Team-wide monthly salary cap of $3,000 in 1926 (both ECL and NNL), reduced to $2,700 by 1927.
Depression-era salary. Approximately $170 per season (likely per month, given context). Salaries dropped to $150/month maximum, down from up to $400 in the 1920s.
Salaries soared during wartime. Journeymen earned $400+/month, stars $1,000/month. Paige earned $30,000-$40,000/year through special arrangements. Black baseball became a $2 million-a-year business.

negro_leagues_daily.notes: Spec cites Larry Lester for $1.00-$1.50/day. This figure is widely cited in Negro Leagues literature but the specific Lester/USA Today December 2020 source could not be independently verified via web search. Multiple secondary sources confirm the general range.

mlb_daily.notes: Spec cites Lester for $15/day MLB meal money. This specific figure could not be independently verified via web search, but the 10:1 ratio between MLB and Negro Leagues meal money is consistent with the broader salary disparity.

lester_salary_tiers.notes: These specific tier figures ($75/$175/$375) are attributed to Larry Lester in the chapter spec. They are consistent with the independently verified ranges ($100-$400 monthly per Phillies UYA, $150 journeyman per Britannica, $230 average per Gardner/Shortelle) but the exact three-tier breakdown could not be independently located in web-accessible sources. The figures are plausible and internally consistent with verified data.
